concepts_addressed: Price as Expectation and Quality Frame

claims.text: People's appreciation of a product's value is entirely determined by context and immediate experience, not by the product's inherent properties. Coca-Cola sold in a refrigerated vending machine commands 6-8x per-unit-volume premium compared to the same product sold at room temperature in a bulk discount format.
claims.speaker: Unknown
claims.topic: Price as Expectation and Quality Frame
claims.mechanism: Context changes perceived value through environmental cues (temperature, format, setting), altering willingness-to-pay without product change.

claims.text: Packaging is a major determinant of perceived value and purchasing choice, shaping the customer experience sufficiently to alter purchase behavior. Water in a bottle elicits rejection; the same water in a can receives positive evaluation.
claims.speaker: Unknown
claims.mechanism: Packaging determines the subjective experience consumers attribute to a product, triggering distinct willingness-to-pay and acceptance responses independent of functional difference.
bounded_by: Does not account for functional differences in packaging (durability, resealability, insulation)

examples.text: Coca-Cola is sold in approximately 12 different retail formats and contexts. A consumer purchasing a cold can from a refrigerated vending machine pays 6-8 times more per unit volume than a consumer buying the same beverage in a room-temperature 2-liter PET bottle from a discount store. The identical product in different packaging and environmental contexts generates radically different willingness-to-pay.
examples.topic: Price as Expectation and Quality Frame

examples.text: Water purchased in a bottle format was rejected by the speaker ('no thanks I'll buy something else'). The identical or chemically similar water presented in a can format was positively received ('actually this is quite a nice part of my hydration repertoire'). Packaging alone—not the product—drove the difference in acceptance.

practices.text: Design product packaging and retail context to align with target customer perception and willingness-to-pay. The same product in different formats commands dramatically different prices.
